claims.flip_evidence_text: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal... provided, however, that no such actions may be taken unless and until: (I) the Company has given Parent written notice at least four Business Days in advance... (II) during the Notice Period... negotiate in good faith... and (III) at the end of the Notice Period... determined in good faith... that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement... continues to be... with respect to a Superior Proposal
claims.notes: Omits that COR requires an unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal (not withdrawn) or an Intervening Event; omits mandatory consultation with outside legal counsel and financial advisor; misattributes the fiduciary-duty determination as the explicit standard for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement; and omits the notice-period, good-faith negotiation, and Agreement-termination prerequisites.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 6.02. Acquisition Proposals; Change of Recommendation. <omitted> (d) No Change of Recommendation or Alternative Acquisition Agreement. <omitted> (iii) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 6.02(d), prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company’s obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, -68- that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal (and the Company may enter into or cause one of its Subsidiaries to enter into such an Alternative Acquisition Agreement) or agree, authorize or commit to do so; provided, however, that no such actions may be taken unless and until: (I) the Company has given Parent written notice at least four Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”), which notice shall set forth in writing that the Company Board intends to consider whether to take such action and a reasonably detailed description of the basis therefor, and shall also include, (y) in the case of such an Acquisition Proposal, all information required by Section 6.02(c), mutatis mutandis, and (z) in the case of an Intervening Event, a reasonably detailed description of such Intervening Event; (II) during the Notice Period, to the extent requested by Parent, the Company shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, negotiate in good faith with Parent to revise this Agreement so that the conditions set forth in clauses (A)(2) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would not be satisfied or such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would no longer be with respect to a Superior Proposal, as applicable; and (III) at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions to this Agreement proposed by Parent in writing in response to such notice contemplated by clause (I) of this 6.02(d)(iii) prior to the end of the Notice Period, and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be (it being understood that (y) any revisions to any Acquisition Proposal shall be deemed to be a new Acquisition Proposal for purposes of Section 6.02(c) and this Section 6.02(d)(iii), including for purposes of the Notice Period, except that subsequent to the initial Notice Period, the Notice Period shall be reduced to two Business Days and (z) prior to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), the Company shall have terminated this Agreement and abandoned the transactions contemplated by this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.03(b)). (Pages 73-74)

Question:
COR standard (superior offer)

Llama answer:
The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

Canonical claims:
- claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 6.02. Acquisition Proposals; Change of Recommendation. <omitted> (d) No Change of Recommendation or Alternative Acquisition Agreement. <omitted> (iii) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 6.02(d), prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company’s obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, -68- that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal (and the Company may enter into or cause one of its Subsidiaries to enter into such an Alternative Acquisition Agreement) or agree, authorize or commit to do so; provided, however, that no such actions may be taken unless and until: (I) the Company has given Parent written notice at least four Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”), which notice shall set forth in writing that the Company Board intends to consider whether to take such action and a reasonably detailed description of the basis therefor, and shall also include, (y) in the case of such an Acquisition Proposal, all information required by Section 6.02(c), mutatis mutandis, and (z) in the case of an Intervening Event, a reasonably detailed description of such Intervening Event; (II) during the Notice Period, to the extent requested by Parent, the Company shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, negotiate in good faith with Parent to revise this Agreement so that the conditions set forth in clauses (A)(2) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would not be satisfied or such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would no longer be with respect to a Superior Proposal, as applicable; and (III) at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions to this Agreement proposed by Parent in writing in response to such notice contemplated by clause (I) of this 6.02(d)(iii) prior to the end of the Notice Period, and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be (it being understood that (y) any revisions to any Acquisition Proposal shall be deemed to be a new Acquisition Proposal for purposes of Section 6.02(c) and this Section 6.02(d)(iii), including for purposes of the Notice Period, except that subsequent to the initial Notice Period, the Notice Period shall be reduced to two Business Days and (z) prior to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), the Company shall have terminated this Agreement and abandoned the transactions contemplated by this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.03(b)). (Pages 73-74)

Question:
COR standard (superior offer)

Llama answer:
The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

Canonical claims:
- claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator. I need to evaluate a specific claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer (Llama answer).

 Let me analyze the claim: 
 "claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Now let me check the contract excerpt carefully.

 From the contract:
 Section 6.02(d)(iii) states:
 Prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company's obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may:
 (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal;

 and/or 

 (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company's Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal...

 So for Change of Recommendation:
 - Need unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal not withdrawn OR Intervening Event
 - Need good faith determination by Board after consultation with outside legal counsel that failure to effect Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties
 - AND in case of Acquisition Proposal, after consultation with financial advisor, that it constitutes a Superior Proposal

 For Alternative Acquisition Agreement:
 - Must be with respect to a Superior Proposal
 - Must follow notice period, good faith negotiation, etc.
 - Must determine it continues to be a Superior Proposal

 The claim says: "if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Is this fully accurate?

 Issues with the claim:
 1. For Change of Recommendation, the Board must also determine that an unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal has been received and not been withdrawn OR an Intervening Event has occurred. The claim omits the requirement of an unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal or Intervening Event.
 2. For Change of Recommendation regarding an Acquisition Proposal, the Board must also consult with outside legal counsel and financial advisor. The claim mentions "determines in good faith" but omits the consultation requirements (outside legal counsel for both, financial advisor for Superior Proposal).
 3. For Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the claim says "if it determines... that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties" - but the contract language for Alternative Acquisition Agreement (clause B) doesn't explicitly say the Board must determine that failure to enter into it would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties as a condition precedent in the same way. Actually, looking at clause (III), at the end of the Notice Period, the Board must determine that the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. The fiduciary duty determination in clause (A)(2) is specific to Change of Recommendation. Wait, let me re-read.

 Actually, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement under clause (B), the conditions are:
 - (I) Notice
 - (II) Good faith negotiation if requested
 - (III) At the end of Notice Period, Board shall have taken into account revisions and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent... OR that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Wait, clause (III) says: "the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions... and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement... continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 So for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board needs to determine that it continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal (after consultation with financial advisor). The fiduciary duty determination is tied to the Change of Recommendation. But the claim groups them together.

 More importantly, the claim is a simplification that omits several key conditions:
 - The requirement that the Acquisition Proposal be unsolicited, bona fide, written, and not withdrawn (for Change of Recommendation)
 - The Intervening Event alternative (for Change of Recommendation)
 - The consultation requirements (outside legal counsel and financial advisor)
 - The notice period and good faith negotiation requirements
 - The termination requirement before entering into Alternative Acquisition Agreement

 However, is the claim "partially true" or "false"? The claim states a condition under which the Board MAY take these actions. It doesn't say "if and only if" or claim these are the only conditions. It says "may... if..."

 But even as a sufficient condition, is it accurate? The claim suggests that the Board may effect a Change of Recommendation if it determines in good faith fiduciary inconsistency AND it constitutes a Superior Proposal. But actually, for Change of Recommendation in case of an Intervening Event, the Superior Proposal requirement doesn't apply! Clause (A)(2) says: "in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) ... that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal". So if it's an Intervening Event (clause (A)(1)(y)), the Board doesn't need to determine it's a Superior Proposal. The claim says "and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal" - this is wrong for Change of Recommendation based on Intervening Event, where no Superior Proposal is needed.

 Also, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the claim includes the fiduciary duty determination, but the text makes clear that at the end of the notice period, the Board must determine either (a) failure to effect Change of Recommendation continues to be inconsistent with fiduciary duties, OR (b) the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. Actually wait, looking at clause (III) again carefully:

 "shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 So for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board needs to determine that it continues to be a Superior Proposal (after financial advisor consultation). The fiduciary duty determination is specifically about "failure to effect a Change of Recommendation". The claim says the Board may enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties. That's not exactly what the text says. The text says for the Alternative Acquisition Agreement path, the Board must determine that the agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Additionally, the claim omits all the procedural requirements (notice, negotiation, etc.).

 Given these inaccuracies:
 1. Superior Proposal is not required for Change of Recommendation based on Intervening Event.
 2. The fiduciary duty language is not exactly the condition for Alternative Acquisition Agreement.
 3. Multiple material conditions are omitted.

 Is this "partially_true" or "false"? Since the claim captures the core substantive standards but misstates/misattributes them and omits key prerequisites, I think "partially_true" is appropriate. It gets the gist that fiduciary duties and Superior Proposal are central, but it conflates the standards and omits material requirements.

 Wait, but looking at the canonical claims list, there's only one claim. And I need to evaluate it.

 Actually, looking at the Llama answer, it matches the claim almost exactly (in fact, it IS the claim). The question is "COR standard (superior offer)" which likely means Change of Recommendation standard regarding a superior offer.

 Let me reconsider whether this could be "true". If we interpret the claim charitably as summarizing the standard for Change of Recommendation in the case of a Superior Proposal (not Intervening Event), and for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, does it hold?

 For Change of Recommendation based on Acquisition Proposal (x):
 - Must determine failure to effect would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties (after legal counsel consultation)
 - Must determine it constitutes Superior Proposal (after financial advisor consultation)
 So that part is true.

 For Alternative Acquisition Agreement:
 - Must be with respect to Superior Proposal
 - But the claim says "if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so [enter into Alternative Acquisition Agreement?] would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties". The contract doesn't explicitly say this for Alternative Acquisition Agreement. However, one could argue that if it's a Superior Proposal and they can enter into it, the fiduciary duty standard is implicit. But the text actually says for clause (III) they must determine that the agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Actually, looking more carefully at the structure:
 Clause (A) allows Change of Recommendation.
 Clause (B) allows entering into Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

 The claim conflates the conditions. The conditions in clause (III) apply to both actions. Clause (III) says at the end of the Notice Period, the Board must determine either:
 - that failure to effect Change of Recommendation continues to be inconsistent with fiduciary duties, OR
 - that the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Wait, this is an "or" at the end! So for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board doesn't need to determine that failure to enter into it is inconsistent with fiduciary duties; it needs to determine that it continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. The fiduciary duty determination is for Change of Recommendation.

 So the claim is wrong to say the Board may enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties. The condition for Alternative Acquisition Agreement is that it continues to be a Superior Proposal.

 Also, the claim says "the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal". An Alternative Acquisition Agreement doesn't "constitute" a Superior Proposal; it is "with respect to" a Superior Proposal.

 Given these textual inaccuracies, I think the claim is partially true at best. It captures some elements but misstates the legal standard for Alternative Acquisition Agreements and omits the Intervening Event exception (where Superior Proposal isn't needed) or alternatively implies Superior Proposal is always needed.

 However, the question is "COR standard (superior offer)". This suggests the question is specifically about the standard when there IS a superior offer. So perhaps the Intervening Event issue is out of scope? But the claim doesn't limit itself to superior offer contexts; it states a general rule.

 Let me think about "load_bearing_label". This asks whether the evidence text (the contract) is load-bearing for the claim. The claim is about the contract terms, so yes, the contract excerpt is load-bearing. If I change the contract text, the claim's truth value would change.

 Actually, looking at the instructions: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This means if the evidence is load-bearing, I need to provide the flip evidence text - presumably a modified version of the evidence that would flip the correctness label? Or is it asking for the evidence text that bears the load? The name suggests text that if flipped (negated/changed) would change the answer. But in many annotation tasks, "flip evidence" means providing the specific sentence or portion of the text that supports or refutes the claim. Wait, the field is "flip_evidence_text". 

 Actually, in some legal annotation frameworks (like those derived from Atticus or similar), "flip evidence" refers to the evidence that determines the flip side - i.e., the specific text from the source that bears on the claim. Or it could mean "the evidence text that, if it were different, would flip the label". Given the name "flip_evidence_text" and the requirement to include it when load_bearing=yes, I think it wants the specific excerpt from the contract that is relevant to evaluating the claim (the evidence that could flip the assessment if interpreted differently).

 But actually, looking at the instruction more carefully: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". I think in this context, "flip evidence text" likely means the specific text from the contract that bears the load (i.e., the text that supports or contradicts the claim). However, the term "flip" suggests it could be the minimal text modification that would flip the label. 

 Wait, I should look at similar annotation tasks. In some contractNLI or similar tasks, "flip" refers to the evidence that if flipped (negated), would change the entailment relationship. But here, given it's a legal annotator, and the field is paired with load_bearing, I think flip_evidence_text is the specific span of text from the contract that is load-bearing (i.e., the evidence itself).

 Let me reconsider. The term might mean: what is the text that determines whether the claim is true or false? If you "flip" that text (i.e., it said the opposite), the claim would be false/true instead. So it's the critical evidence text.

 Given the contract is the only evidence provided, and the claim directly references it, load_bearing_label is definitely yes.

 For flip_evidence_text, I should provide the specific clauses from the contract that are most relevant. Given the claim's inaccuracies, the flip evidence should probably include the text that shows the requirements the claim missed or misstated. But actually, since the claim is a positive claim about what the contract says, the load-bearing text is the text that addresses Change of Recommendation and Alternative Acquisition Agreement standards.

 Actually, I think the safest interpretation is that flip_evidence_text should contain the specific excerpt(s) from the contract that are load-bearing for the assessment. I'll provide the key relevant text spans.

 But wait - could the claim be considered true? Let me reconsider.

 The claim: "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Is this a fair summary? 
 - The contract does say the Board may effect a Change of Recommendation if it determines in good faith that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties (after consultation with counsel) and (in case of Acquisition Proposal) that it constitutes a Superior Proposal (after consultation with financial advisor).
 - The contract does say the Board may enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 However, the claim makes it sound like the fiduciary duty determination AND Superior Proposal determination are both required for both actions. But:
 1. For Change of Recommendation based on Intervening Event, no Superior Proposal is needed.
 2. For Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the explicit determination at the end is that it continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal, not necessarily that failure to enter it is inconsistent with fiduciary duties (though that's implicit, the text uses an "or" structure in clause III referencing either fiduciary duty for COR or Superior Proposal for AAA).

 Given that the question is "COR standard (superior offer)", it's focused on Change of Recommendation standard when dealing with a superior offer. The claim mentions both COR and AAA. 

 I think "partially_true" is the right label because the claim captures the general idea but conflates and oversimplifies the standards, omitting key distinctions and procedural requirements.

 Wait, I should check if there's a standard that claims in these tasks are either true or false, and partially_true is only for very specific cases. But the instructions say true, false, or partially_true. So partially_true is available and appropriate here.

 Actually, looking at the Llama answer, it is identical to the claim. The task is to evaluate the canonical claim, not necessarily the Llama answer, but the Llama answer is provided as context. The claim is exactly the Llama answer.

 Let me reconsider whether the claim is actually true. If we read it as "The Company Board may [do X or Y] if [condition 1] and [condition 2]", does the contract support that the Board may do this under those conditions? Yes, those conditions are part of the requirements. The claim doesn't say "if and only if" or "if [and only if]". It states sufficient conditions. Are the conditions stated sufficient? Well, they're not sufficient because other requirements exist (notice, etc.). But are they necessary conditions? For Change of Recommendation based on Acquisition Proposal, yes (fiduciary + Superior Proposal). For AAA, Superior Proposal is necessary.

 However, the claim is misleading because:
 - It omits that for COR based on Intervening Event, Superior Proposal is NOT required.
 - It attributes the fiduciary duty determination to AAA explicitly when the text structures it differently.
 - It omits the "unsolicited, bona fide written" and "not withdrawn" requirements for the Acquisition Proposal leading to COR.

 These are material omissions/misstatements in a legal context. "Partially_true" seems right.

 For load_bearing: yes, the contract text is load-bearing.

 For flip_evidence_text: I need to provide text from the contract that demonstrates the partial truth / issues. The key text is the full clause (A) and (B) with conditions. I'll extract the most relevant portion.

 Actually, I think I should provide the specific text that shows the full requirements. Let me draft the flip_evidence_text carefully. It should probably be the exact text from the contract that bears on the claim.

 I'll provide:
 "the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal..."

 But maybe I should also include clause (III) to show the "or" structure. Let me include enough to show the distinction.

 Actually, looking at the exact wording of the claim again, I want to make sure. The claim says "and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal." This is imprecise because an Alternative Acquisition Agreement is entered into "with respect to" a Superior Proposal, not that the agreement itself "constitutes" a Superior Proposal. But that's minor.

 More importantly, the claim says the Board may effect a COR "if it determines... that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties... and the Acquisition Proposal... constitutes a Superior Proposal." This accurately reflects clause (A)(2) for Acquisition Proposals. But it omits the Intervening Event path entirely. Is omitting an alternative path enough to make a claim partially true? If the claim implies this is the only way, then yes. If the claim is just describing one way, it might be true. But typically in these tasks, if a claim states a condition without qualification, and there's another unmentioned condition that also permits the action, the claim is considered incomplete/misleading.

 Given the question is specifically "COR standard (superior offer)", the claim is likely intended to capture the standard when dealing with a superior offer, not an Intervening Event. But the claim explicitly mentions Alternative Acquisition Agreement too, which complicates things.

 I think I'll go with partially_true.

 Wait, let me reconsider one more time. Maybe the claim is actually true under a generous reading. The claim doesn't say these are the ONLY conditions. It says the Board MAY do X if [conditions]. In logic, "if P then Q" is true even if there are other conditions R, S, T that are also required, as long as whenever the conditions in P are met (and presumably others?), Q is permitted. But actually, if R, S, T are also necessary, then P alone isn't sufficient, so saying "if P" is misleading because it suggests P is sufficient. However, in natural language, people often say "You can do X if Y" when Y is a key condition among others.

 But in legal annotation tasks (like ContractNLI or similar), the standard is usually strict. If a claim omits material conditions, it's considered false or partially true.

 Let me look at the structure. This looks like it could be from an evaluation of LLM-generated claims against contracts, similar to the Atticus work or LegalBench. In those contexts, claims that are "mostly right but oversimplified" are often labeled partially_true if they capture the main gist but miss nuances, or false if they miss material terms.

 I think partially_true is safest.

 Let me finalize:
 - correctness_label: partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: The key contract text showing (A) requires either an unsolicited bona fide Acquisition Proposal or an Intervening Event, plus good-faith fiduciary determination and (for Acquisition Proposal) Superior Proposal finding, while (B) requires the agreement be with respect to a Superior Proposal, and clause (III) requires notice, negotiation, and a final determination that either fiduciary duties continue to require a COR or the AAA continues to relate to a Superior Proposal. I'll include a substantial quote.
 - notes: something brief about omitted conditions (unsolicited bona fide proposal/Intervening Event distinction, consultation requirements, notice/negotiation) and misattribution of fiduciary-duty test to AAA.

 Actually, for flip_evidence_text, I should quote the specific contradictory or qualifying text. The claim implies Superior Proposal is always required for COR, but the text says "(y) Intervening Event has occurred" without requiring Superior Proposal. So I should include that.

 Let me analyze the claim: 
 "claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Now let me check the contract excerpt carefully.

 From the contract:
 Section 6.02(d)(iii) states:
 Prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company's obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may:
 (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal;

 and/or 

 (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company's Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal...

 So for Change of Recommendation:
 - Need unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal not withdrawn OR Intervening Event
 - Need good faith determination by Board after consultation with outside legal counsel that failure to effect Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties
 - AND in case of Acquisition Proposal, after consultation with financial advisor, that it constitutes a Superior Proposal

 For Alternative Acquisition Agreement:
 - Must be with respect to a Superior Proposal
 - Must follow notice period, good faith negotiation, etc.
 - Must determine it continues to be a Superior Proposal

 The claim says: "if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Is this fully accurate?

 Issues with the claim:
 1. For Change of Recommendation, the Board must also determine that an unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal has been received and not been withdrawn OR an Intervening Event has occurred. The claim omits the requirement of an unsolicited bona fide written Acquisition Proposal or Intervening Event.
 2. For Change of Recommendation regarding an Acquisition Proposal, the Board must also consult with outside legal counsel and financial advisor. The claim mentions "determines in good faith" but omits the consultation requirements (outside legal counsel for both, financial advisor for Superior Proposal).
 3. For Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the claim says "if it determines... that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties" - but the contract language for Alternative Acquisition Agreement (clause B) doesn't explicitly say the Board must determine that failure to enter into it would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties as a condition precedent in the same way. Actually, looking at clause (III), at the end of the Notice Period, the Board must determine that the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. The fiduciary duty determination in clause (A)(2) is specific to Change of Recommendation. Wait, let me re-read.

 Actually, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement under clause (B), the conditions are:
 - (I) Notice
 - (II) Good faith negotiation if requested
 - (III) At the end of Notice Period, Board shall have taken into account revisions and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent... OR that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Wait, clause (III) says: "the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions... and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement... continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 So for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board needs to determine that it continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal (after consultation with financial advisor). The fiduciary duty determination is tied to the Change of Recommendation. But the claim groups them together.

 More importantly, the claim is a simplification that omits several key conditions:
 - The requirement that the Acquisition Proposal be unsolicited, bona fide, written, and not withdrawn (for Change of Recommendation)
 - The Intervening Event alternative (for Change of Recommendation)
 - The consultation requirements (outside legal counsel and financial advisor)
 - The notice period and good faith negotiation requirements
 - The termination requirement before entering into Alternative Acquisition Agreement

 However, is the claim "partially true" or "false"? The claim states a condition under which the Board MAY take these actions. It doesn't say "if and only if" or claim these are the only conditions. It says "may... if..."

 But even as a sufficient condition, is it accurate? The claim suggests that the Board may effect a Change of Recommendation if it determines in good faith fiduciary inconsistency AND it constitutes a Superior Proposal. But actually, for Change of Recommendation in case of an Intervening Event, the Superior Proposal requirement doesn't apply! Clause (A)(2) says: "in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) ... that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal". So if it's an Intervening Event (clause (A)(1)(y)), the Board doesn't need to determine it's a Superior Proposal. The claim says "and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal" - this is wrong for Change of Recommendation based on Intervening Event, where no Superior Proposal is needed.

 Also, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the claim includes the fiduciary duty determination, but the text makes clear that at the end of the notice period, the Board must determine either (a) failure to effect Change of Recommendation continues to be inconsistent with fiduciary duties, OR (b) the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. Actually wait, looking at clause (III) again carefully:

 "shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 So for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board needs to determine that it continues to be a Superior Proposal (after financial advisor consultation). The fiduciary duty determination is specifically about "failure to effect a Change of Recommendation". The claim says the Board may enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties. That's not exactly what the text says. The text says for the Alternative Acquisition Agreement path, the Board must determine that the agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal.

 Additionally, the claim omits all the procedural requirements (notice, negotiation, etc.).
